_comment: Kitsoki concept pitch. Leads with control inversion and the proofs a coding-agent skeptic can't dismiss: the runtime overrules the model mid-call, edits hot-reload and replay byte-for-byte as tests, and the workflow runs where work already lives. After the one-prompt strawman it credits the Cherny loop (loop engineering: a gated, budgeted agent loop that converges) and argues phases-not-prompts is that idea generalized into a composable graph. The phases arc then shows the gates routing review failures back to implement, plan, or reproduce — and that this can loop multiple times.

theme.name: rose-pine-moon

scenes.subtitleHtml: A conversational workflow engine built for reliable deliveryProduction-ready workflows. Evidence-first execution. Reliable handoff.

scenes.body: Processes are a complex adaptive system.
scenes.lede: People, agents, tools, artifacts, failures, and feedback loops keep perturbing each other.
scenes.narration: The Software Development Lifecycle is a complex adaptive system: people, agents, tools, artifacts, failures, and feedback loops.

scenes.title: Root 1: General System Theory

books.title: General System Theory
books.subtitle: Foundations, Development, Applications
books.takeaway: Open systems exchange matter, energy, and information with an environment.

scenes.caption: Systems are structural: behavior lives in the organization of the whole and how the parts interact, the whole is more than the sum of the parts.
scenes.narration: General System Theory frames systems as open, organized, and environment-coupled.

scenes.lede: The effectiveness of processes is in the structure, and the management of the relationship with the open environment.
scenes.narration: Kitsoki makes the boundary explicit: state in, artifacts out, feedback always attached.

scenes.title: Root 2: Design in Nature

books.title: Design in Nature
books.subtitle: How the Constructal Law Governs Evolution
books.authors: Adrian Bejan and J. Peder Zane
books.takeaway: Persistent systems evolve structures that make flow easier.

scenes.caption: The constructal law says durable systems change shape to improve access to the currents moving through them.
scenes.narration: Constructal law adds the second root: systems endure by improving flow architecture.

scenes.lede: Structured stories provide the channels that accelerate flow and can be reshaped over time.
scenes.narration: Kitsoki makes flow visible so repeated friction can be removed instead of hidden.

scenes.title: Root 3: The Tree of Knowledge

books.title: The Tree of Knowledge
books.subtitle: The Biological Roots of Human Understanding
books.authors: Humberto R. Maturana and Francisco J. Varela
books.takeaway: Knowing is enacted through a self-maintaining loop between agent and environment.

scenes.caption: Maturana and Varela treat cognition as embodied action: a living system keeps itself going by coupling to its world. The observer is part of the system.
scenes.narration: The Tree of Knowledge adds the third root: cognition is a self-maintaining loop.

scenes.lede: The agent, artifact, and environment keep shaping each other over time, so the runtime has to preserve continuity.
scenes.narration: Kitsoki preserves the loop so work can be inspected, replayed, tested, and improved.

scenes.title: Three roots, one runtime
scenes.caption: Kitsoki makes the three roots executable: open systems define the boundary, constructal law improves flow, and cognition preserves the loop.
scenes.narration: Kitsoki makes the three roots executable: open systems define the boundary, constructal law improves flow, and cognition preserves the loop.

scenes.title: Continuous improvement needs a process
scenes.caption: Lean and Six Sigma work because the process is explicit enough to observe, measure, change, and hold stable.
scenes.narration: Continuous improvement needs a process. Lean and Six Sigma work because the process is explicit enough to measure, change, and hold stable.

scenes.title: Prompts are not enough
scenes.caption: Prompts can improve the agent. Structured workflows improve the system around the agent.
scenes.narration: Prompts can improve agent behavior, but behavior is hard to control with prompts alone. Structured workflows give us phases, gates, traces, and tests.

scenes.title: Every decision becomes training data
scenes.caption: Because every decision is recorded with its operator, input, and output, a running story creates its own supervised dataset.
scenes.narration: Every Kitsoki decision is recorded with the operator, input, and output. That means a running story produces labeled training data as a side effect.

scenes.title: Train the story like a model
scenes.caption: The learned behavior lives in git as YAML, tests, and knowledge, not in opaque model weights.
scenes.narration: Training a Kitsoki story means reading the trace, promoting repeated decisions into story structure, replaying the result, and keeping the learned behavior in git.

scenes.body: You have a workflow. People drive it by chatting where the work already lives.
scenes.lede: A GitHub issue. A Jira ticket. A Slack thread. A CRM record.
scenes.narration: You have a workflow. People drive it by chatting where the work lives.

scenes.title: One workflow, many surfaces
scenes.caption: One YAML state machine drives every surface — the same flow runs in a terminal and in a real ticket thread.
scenes.narration: One YAML state machine drives every surface. GitHub, Jira, Slack, or your terminal.

scenes.body: Building this chatbot is harder than it looks.
scenes.lede: Pure LLM agents drift, can't be tested, and break unpredictably. Hand-coded state machines can't parse free text or evolve.
scenes.narration: Building this is harder than it looks. Agents drift. State machines can't parse text.

scenes.title: Cat wrangling
scenes.narration: Orchestrating agents without structure is cat wrangling. Kitsoki ends the wrangling by keeping the runtime in charge.

panels.label: Without structure
panels.caption: The work is improvised in the moment, so drift only shows up after the damage is done.

panels.label: With Kitsoki
panels.caption: The runtime keeps the plan explicit, the work bounded, and the feedback attached.

scenes.caption: Kitsoki removes the wrangling by putting the runtime between intent and action.

scenes.title: Control inversion

panels.label: Typical agent
panels.caption: LLM holds the plan; runtime executes.

panels.label: Kitsoki
panels.caption: Runtime holds the plan; LLM is called as a sub-task.

scenes.caption: The arrow that matters is the LLM's return arrow — a typed intent, not a free-form action.
scenes.narration: Kitsoki flips control. The runtime is in charge. The LLM is called only when interpretation is needed.

scenes.title: One prompt
scenes.caption: One agent. One prompt. No structure between the steps.
scenes.narration: The naive approach: one big prompt. Validate, fix, verify. The agent does everything.

scenes.title: The Cherny loop

nodes.label: goal
nodes.sub: + a gate that can prove it

nodes.label: done
nodes.sub: goal met — or budget ceiling hit

scenes.caption: Loop engineering, after Boris Cherny: an agent iterates toward a goal until a gate proves it's actually met — or a budget ceiling stops it. Launch it once; it converges on its own.
scenes.narration: A Cherny loop gives an agent a goal, checker, and budget. Each failed gate feeds the next try.

scenes.body: Kitsoki the conclusion of this idea — a graph of these loops.
scenes.lede: A Cherny loop solves one goal with one gate and one budget. Kitsoki turns each phase into a separate gated loop, then composes those loops into a durable workflow that can be reused and improved.
scenes.narration: Kitsoki generalizes that loop: every phase gets a gate and budget, then composes into a graph.

scenes._comment: Scene 7–13: full graph shown throughout; one item highlighted per slide.
scenes.title: Phases, not prompts
scenes.caption: Phase 1: read-only — confirm the bug exists before any code changes.
scenes.narration: Four phases, three gates — starting with reproduce.

scenes.title: Phases, not prompts
scenes.caption: Deterministic gate: the test must fail first — no planning until the bug is proven reproducible. A script decides, not a judgment.
scenes.narration: The first gate: test must fail before planning begins.

scenes.title: Phases, not prompts
scenes.caption: Phase 2: propose the fix approach — requires operator sign-off before proceeding.
scenes.narration: Plan proposes the fix — awaiting operator sign-off.

scenes.title: Phases, not prompts
scenes.caption: Gate: no code written until the plan is approved.
scenes.narration: Gate: no code changes until the plan is signed off.

scenes.title: Phases, not prompts
scenes.caption: Phase 3: writes to an isolated worktree — scoped to code changes only.
scenes.narration: Implement writes to a scoped worktree — isolated from main.

scenes.title: Phases, not prompts
scenes.caption: Deterministic gate: the tests must pass — review only sees working code. A script decides, for free.
scenes.narration: Gate: tests must pass before sending to human review.

scenes.title: Phases, not prompts
scenes.caption: Layer 1 is mechanical: the gate just runs the tests. They fail — or won't even run against the harness — and implement retries itself, in place. No human, no step backward.
scenes.narration: Tests fail? Implement loops in place and fixes the code.

scenes.title: Phases, not prompts
scenes.caption: The last gate is different: review is an agent — an adversarial judgment, not a script. It can approve the work, or send it back.
scenes.narration: Agent review can approve or send the work back.

scenes.title: Phases, not prompts
scenes.caption: Three deterministic gates (orange) are scripts — exact, free, incorruptible. The last gate is an agent (violet): an adversarial review for what no test can encode. Pass, and it merges.
scenes.narration: Most gates are deterministic scripts. The final gate is agent review for quality no test can encode.

scenes.title: Phases, not prompts
scenes.caption: Just a missed case — back to implement. The plan and root cause stand.
scenes.narration: Missed edge case: back to implement. Same plan, same diagnosis.

scenes.title: Phases, not prompts
scenes.caption: The fix was wrong — back to plan. Re-plan against what review learned.
scenes.narration: Wrong plan: back to plan and choose another approach.

scenes.title: Phases, not prompts
scenes.caption: The root cause was wrong — back to reproduce. Investigate before planning again.
scenes.narration: Wrong root cause: back to reproduce before planning again.

scenes._comment: P1 — the host rejects the model mid-call. The single hardest beat for a coding-agent skeptic to dismiss: their model is its own authority.
scenes.title: The runtime overrules the model
scenes.caption: Even when the LLM is called, the runtime validates the answer — and can reject and re-prompt it mid-call.
scenes.narration: When the LLM is called, the runtime polices it. Off-contract decisions are rejected; valid ones hit the trace.

scenes.lede: Edit the YAML and the running machine hot-reloads. Each turn replays byte-for-byte without an LLM call — the spec itself is the regression test. Attach it to GitHub Issues or Jira with one config change.
scenes.narration: Author locally. Edits hot-reload, turns replay without the LLM, then attach GitHub or Jira.

scenes.title: Driven from where work lives

messages.author: alice

• added negative test for service-account JWT
• tightened middleware match

Acknowledged. Pushing fix…

✓ pushed  a3f8d2c · refactor(auth): use ternary in service-account check

scenes.caption: No local checkout. No "kitsoki run". The state machine runs in CI; comments are the surface.
scenes.narration: Developers drive workflows from where the work lives. Comment on a ticket to refine. Comment on a pull request to push a fix.

scenes.body: Use it the way a customer would.
scenes.lede: Start by onboarding the repo through Kitsoki MCP. Read the status and next steps. Then drive planning, bugfix, git ops, and story improvement through the same governed surface. The bakeoff and harness slides are the proof that the loop works.
scenes.narration: The dogfood path is the customer path: onboard through MCP, read the readiness status, drive real tasks, then use the bakeoff harness as proof.

scenes.title: Onboard through MCP
scenes.prompt: use kitsoki-mcp-drive to onboard gears-rust, infer the project profile, prove the workflow can render, route, and validate before we spend on live work

calls.result: finds active repos, stories, and handoffs

calls.args: "onboard gears-rust and draft the project profile"

lines: inferred rust stack, make dev/test/build, and repo identity
found a stale-binary validator bug and fixed KITSOKI_BIN support
next external LLM draft is gated by explicit approval or a private provider

scenes.caption: Onboarding is a workflow run with artifacts and gates, not a prompt preamble.
scenes.narration: Onboarding itself is driven through MCP. It produces a status, artifacts, a trace, and concrete next steps before expensive work starts.

scenes.title: Onboarding status

items.label: Discovery path
items.detail: gears-rust resolved to a Rust project with make dev, make test, and make build

items.label: MCP live draft
items.detail: host.agent.decide returned a schema-shaped project profile through the live Studio MCP path

items.label: Validator bug
items.detail: init_validate_profile.py now honors KITSOKI_BIN so live dogfood can use current code

items.label: Live continuation
items.detail: rerun the draft with approved repo-data sharing or a local/private model provider

scenes.caption: This is the honest onboarding readout: what is proven, what was fixed, and what is still gated.
scenes.narration: The onboarding status is honest: discovery and profile drafting worked, one environment bug was fixed, and the next live LLM call needs explicit approval or a private provider.

scenes.title: Onboarding next steps

items.label: Promote replay
items.detail: turn the successful discovery and validator-fix path into a no-LLM flow fixture

items.label: Private-provider path
items.detail: rerun profile drafting against a local/private model for sensitive repo data

items.label: Readiness gate
items.detail: block live marathons until profile, test command, worktree policy, and oracle plan are explicit

items.label: Cheaper MCP reads
items.detail: teach drivers to prefer session.status and session.world before heavy inspect

scenes.caption: The next step is not more demo polish. It is turning onboarding into a reusable readiness gate for every repo.

scenes.title: Use it from Claude Code
scenes.prompt: use kitsoki-mcp-drive to onboard the repo, show readiness, then drive the next task through the right Kitsoki story

calls.result: shows active repos, sessions, stories, and follow-ups

calls.args: "plan, fix, commit, and file workflow gaps"
calls.result: routes natural language into story phases
keeps proof for review and training

lines: the runtime chooses phases, gates, tools, and artifacts
failures become traceable follow-up work instead of lost context

scenes.caption: After onboarding, the same MCP surface drives the day-to-day work.
scenes.narration: After onboarding, the same MCP surface drives planning, bugfix, git operations, and story improvement.

scenes.title: Planning through MCP
scenes.prompt: use kitsoki-mcp-drive agent to propose and implement the spatial oracle feature I described in the markdown

calls.args: "propose and implement the spatial oracle feature..."

calls.result: reads state and allowed intents cheaply

lines: approval gate separates design from implementation
trace records why the plan exists

scenes.title: Bugfix through MCP
scenes.prompt: fix the flaky screenshot diff bug from the issue, prove the regression red first, then ship the smallest safe change

lines: review sees a working diff instead of an agent claim

scenes.title: Git operations through MCP
scenes.prompt: merge the finished work back to main, handle conflicts if needed, commit only my changes, and leave unrelated dirt alone

calls.args: "merge the finished work back to main..."

lines: dirty unrelated files are not reverted
operator can reacquire any blocked step

scenes.title: Self-improvement loop
scenes.prompt: this story stalled after the operator chose an on-disk ticket; file a GitHub issue with the trace and a screenshot so we can fix the workflow

calls.result: captures the stale transition and host calls
new issue appears as follow-up work

lines: the agent cannot silently drop MCP gaps
evidence is bundled at filing time
the bug becomes input to the next story improvement run

scenes.title: Story improvement harness
scenes.prompt: run the story improvement harness over failed traces, promote the fixed behavior into replay tests, and show the story diff that makes the next run better

calls.result: collects failures and candidate story diff
flow fixtures replay without LLM spend

lines: prompt and gate changes are tested like code
the next marathon starts from a stronger workflow

scenes.caption: The harness trains stories with traces, gates, and replayable proof, not isolated prompt snippets.

scenes.title: How runs become reusable tests
scenes.caption: A live run is only useful if we can replay it. We turn each real failure into a test case, score future attempts, and use the results to improve the story.
scenes.narration: A live run becomes useful when we can replay it. Each real failure becomes a test case, the harness scores future attempts, and the result improves the story.

scenes.title: The harness contract
scenes.caption: A cell is hermetic and reproducible. If the fixture is not armed, it cannot enter the live runner.
scenes.narration: The harness contract is simple. Prove the baseline is red, withhold the oracle, drive one hermetic cell, then score the result.

scenes.body: Spend only after the case is armed.
scenes.lede: The free path verifies fixtures, preflights candidates, prepares worktrees, renders exact drive commands, and validates the repo-bakeoff story flow. Live LLM cells are the explicit gated step, not the default test path.
scenes.narration: The important process change is no-cost first. Verify and prepare everything before spending on a live cell.

scenes.title: gears-rust marathon
scenes.caption: Ten real gears-rust fixes went through the pipeline and passed independent oracle verification; the corpus now backs repeatable harness checks.
scenes.narration: The gears-rust marathon shipped and independently verified ten out of ten real fixes, then captured the evidence as a reusable corpus.

scenes.title: gears-rust bugs 1-5
scenes.caption: First half of the gears-rust marathon: five pinned real fixes, five independent oracle passes.
scenes.narration: The first half of the gears-rust marathon is traceable to concrete cases, packages, real fix commits, and independent oracle passes.

scenes.title: gears-rust bugs 6-10
scenes.caption: Second half: the same pipeline shape held across five more packages, with no human edits to the fixes.
scenes.narration: The second half kept the same pipeline shape across five more packages, again with independent oracle passes and no human edits to the fixes.

scenes.title: Hard cases made the harness honest
scenes.caption: The stress run did not inflate the score. It exposed one incomplete fix and one stalled maker call, then turned both into generic pipeline hardening.
scenes.narration: The hard-case run was useful because it failed honestly. Two passed, one failed, one stalled, and both problems became generic pipeline fixes.

scenes.title: hard-case stress bugs
scenes.caption: The stress bakeoff stayed honest: H1 and H4 became product hardening, not hidden score inflation.
scenes.narration: The hard-case bakeoff is traceable too. Two passed, one failed with the wrong signal, and one stalled because the runner lacked a watchdog.

scenes.title: query-string live bakeoff
scenes.caption: The query-string run is small but complete: three external parser bugs, three hidden-oracle passes, full suite green each time.
scenes.narration: For query-string, the GPT five point five Kitsoki run solved three out of three cells, with oracle and suite passing each time.

scenes.title: query-string bug traceability
scenes.caption: Each query-string cell starts from the real fix parent and is graded by the real regression test kept out of the maker tree.
scenes.narration: The query-string bakeoff is also traceable: three real filed bugs or pull requests, three pinned baselines, and hidden regression-test grading.

scenes.title: Failure modes became product features
scenes.caption: The harness is built from scars: every recurring failure mode became a deterministic check, a runtime rule, or a story prompt change.
scenes.narration: The harness is not theory. It is a list of failure modes we turned into product behavior.

scenes.title: Stories train like models
scenes.caption: The training example is not a prompt snippet. It is the whole loop: trace, oracle, outcome, story diff, and replayable proof.
scenes.narration: Stories train like models, but the training example is operational: trace, oracle, outcome, story diff, and replayable proof.

scenes.title: What the evidence says

items.label: gears-rust marathon
items.detail: ten real merged-fix baselines re-solved through Kitsoki, each independently verified by hidden PR oracles

items.label: query-string bakeoff
items.detail: three external parser bugs solved by gpt-5.5 through Kitsoki with oracle and suite pass

items.label: hard-case stress
items.detail: multi-file cases passed, H1 exposed near-side assertion risk, H4 exposed a stalled-call watchdog gap

items.label: methodology
items.detail: RED baseline, hidden oracle, hermetic worktree, explicit trace, score before story hardening

scenes.caption: The proof is credible because misses are preserved as product findings, not edited out of the score.

scenes.title: What becomes possible

items.label: Repo readiness
items.detail: onboarding becomes a gate: profile, commands, worktree policy, story route, oracle plan

items.label: Model ladders
items.detail: run cheap-to-expensive candidates and stop once a hidden oracle reaches solved

items.label: Story training
items.detail: a failed run produces trace evidence, a story diff, and replay coverage for the next run

items.label: Customer proof
items.detail: the same harness can answer: should I trust Kitsoki on my repo, with my tests, at this cost?

scenes.caption: This is the customer story: Kitsoki is not a smarter prompt. It is a measurable improvement loop around real work.

scenes.body: The demo becomes an operating system for improvement.
scenes.lede: A customer can onboard a repo, run governed tasks through MCP, score real outcomes against hidden oracles, and watch misses turn into story/runtime changes. That is the thing prompts alone do not give them.
scenes.narration: The demo becomes an operating system for improvement: onboard, drive, score, learn, and rerun.

scenes.title: Improves with use

messages.author: kitsoki
messages.body:
Test-review converted from LLM call → deterministic script.

Trace showed 47 identical calls; replaced with a 12-line script.

messages.author: alice
messages.body: feedback for kitsoki — you're being way too verbose in the comments

messages.body: Acknowledged. Tuning comment verbosity in next deploy.

messages.author: kitsoki
messages.body:
Indexed your team's internal tooling and docs:
• runbook RUN-042 → embedded in the auth-fix prompt
• stand-validate command → wrapped as a host call

scenes.caption: The trace tells the author where the LLM keeps being asked the same question. Each conversion is one diff against the YAML — and the system absorbs your existing tools as it goes.
scenes.narration: Kitsoki improves with use. It converts repeated LLM calls into scripts. It accepts your feedback. And it indexes your team's existing tools.

scenes.title: Lessons Learned
scenes.narration: Lessons learned.

scenes.title: Demo-driven development
scenes.caption: A proposal is not done when the code compiles. It is done when the product can demonstrate the full behavior and the evidence passes review.
scenes.narration: The development pattern is proposal, implementation, deterministic demo, visual QA, then either fix the gap or ship the proof.

scenes.title: The two-skill loop

items.label: Feature spec
items.detail: the proposal or change summary defines what the viewer must see

items.label: kitsoki-ui-demo
items.detail: records deterministic, no-LLM product video plus frames and chapters

items.label: kitsoki-ui-qa
items.detail: judges the video against scenarios, cites frames, and can downgrade passes

items.label: Iteration
items.detail: a QA failure becomes maker feedback, not a hand-waved demo problem

scenes.caption: The demo and the QA report become part of development, not a marketing pass after development.

scenes.title: What the loop produces

items.label: Product videos
items.detail: feature tours are recorded from the real web UI at watch speed

items.label: Feature pressure
items.detail: if the video cannot show the proposal, the product is not legible enough yet

items.label: Reusable fixtures
items.detail: real traces can become no-LLM flows and host cassettes for repeatable demos

items.label: Site content
items.detail: the same proof assets feed the product site, docs, decks, and release review

scenes.caption: The loop turns feature development into evidence production: working UI, deterministic video, QA report, and public-facing proof.

scenes.tagline: Prove what the model did — and what it couldn't
scenes.narration: Kitsoki — prove what the model did, and what it couldn't.
